title: Classifying a Triangle with Sides 5 cm, 5 cm, 8 cm

steps.instruction: Equilateral = all 3 sides equal. Isosceles = exactly 2 sides equal. Scalene = no sides equal.
steps.work: 3 equal → equilateral. 2 equal → isosceles. 0 equal → scalene.
steps.explanation: We classify by sides first by comparing all three side lengths.

steps.instruction: Compare the three sides: 5 cm, 5 cm, 8 cm.
steps.work: 5 = 5, but 5 ≠ 8. Two sides are equal → isosceles.
steps.explanation: Exactly two sides share the same length (5 cm), so this is an isosceles triangle.

steps.instruction: Acute = all angles less than 90°. Right = one angle exactly 90°. Obtuse = one angle greater than 90°.
steps.work: All < 90° → acute. One = 90° → right. One > 90° → obtuse.
steps.explanation: We classify by angles by checking whether any angle is 90° or greater.

steps.instruction: We are told all angles are less than 90°.
steps.explanation: Since no angle reaches 90°, the triangle is acute.

steps.instruction: Combine both classifications into a single description.
steps.work: This is an acute isosceles triangle.
steps.explanation: We describe triangles using both their angle type and side type together.

title: Classifying a Triangle with Sides 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm and a Right Angle

steps.instruction: Compare the three sides: 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm.
steps.work: 3 ≠ 4 ≠ 5, and 3 ≠ 5. No sides are equal → scalene.
steps.explanation: All three side lengths are different, making this a scalene triangle.

steps.instruction: We are told one angle is exactly 90°.
steps.explanation: A triangle with a 90° angle is always called a right triangle.

steps.instruction: The sides 3, 4, 5 are a well-known set. Check: does 3² + 4² = 5²?
steps.explanation: When the squares of the two shorter sides add up to the square of the longest side, the triangle has a right angle. This is related to the Pythagorean theorem.

steps.work: This is a right scalene triangle.
steps.explanation: It has a right angle (90°) and all three sides are different lengths.
